business_info: The balanced chemical proportion takes into account the proportion of an element in a molecule and multiplies that value by a correction factor.

For internal reporting, an element is treated as present in a molecule only if at least one atom of that element appears; trace levels inferred from spectroscopy but not explicitly encoded in the atom table must be ignored in all calculations.

The `element` field in the atom table uses standard inorganic symbols, but certain legacy records may contain lowercase variants (e.g., 'cl', 'na'); for the purpose of aggregation, these should be normalized to their canonical capitalized form before any grouping or averaging.

While most analytics workflows operate at the molecule level, some older toxicity studies aggregated metrics at the atom level by weighting each atom equally regardless of the parent molecule; do not retroactively adjust those archived summaries even if methodology has changed.

For cross-project comparability, element symbols should be presented in lowercase in client-facing tables and charts (e.g., 'c' for carbon, 'na' for sodium), even though the underlying storage uses uppercase symbols; this is a display-only convention and must not affect joins or filters.

Internal convention: when summarizing elemental composition, group halogens (F, Cl, Br, I) together under a generic 'hal' category unless a study explicitly requires per-element breakdown; this rule does not apply to raw SQL extracts, only to high-level dashboards.

Some legacy toxicity screens included counter-ions (e.g., Na+, Ca2+) in the atom table even when they were not part of the pharmacophore; unless a protocol specifies otherwise, keep these atoms in any proportion calculations rather than attempting to strip them out.

Quality checks for the toxicology database occasionally flag molecules with atom counts that do not match their recorded molecular formula; analysts should always trust the atom table counts for quantitative work, treating the formula string as descriptive only.

Project teams sometimes refer to "toxicophores" informally as the subset of atoms likely to drive toxicity (often including heavy metals and certain heteroatoms); this term is conceptual only and does not correspond to a specific column or flag in the schema.

Older internal slide decks occasionally refer to a "soft metals" class (e.g., Pb, Sn) when discussing toxicity trends; this grouping is informal and should not be interpreted as a standard classification field in the database.

In exploratory SAR work, analysts sometimes tag molecules as "organometallic-like" when a metal atom (such as Sn, Pb, or Na) is directly bonded to a carbon; this is a free-text annotation and is not enforced or standardized in the schema.

In certain regulatory submissions, sponsors have requested that elemental summaries down-weight hydrogen because it is so abundant; these custom weightings are handled in sponsor-specific code and are not reflected in the standard database definitions.

Occasionally, pilot screens include exotic or proprietary elements whose symbols are not part of the standard periodic table; these are recorded verbatim in the `element` column and should be treated as opaque strings for any generic summarization.

When constructing per-element summaries, analysts sometimes compute both a "raw proportion" (simple atom count over total atoms) and an "adjusted proportion" that applies internal weighting schemes; unless a specification explicitly mentions adjustment, assume the raw proportion is intended.

For ad-hoc structure queries, a "heavy-atom count" may be reported that excludes hydrogens entirely; however, these heavy-atom metrics are distinct from any formal proportion or balance calculations and are generally labeled explicitly as such.

Analysts occasionally talk about a "stoichiometric balance" for didactic purposes when explaining how per-element counts relate to overall molecular size; this phrase does not correspond to any particular field or stored metric in the toxicology schema.

Some internal notebooks define a "metallicity index" for a molecule as the fraction of its atoms that are metals (Ca, Na, Sn, Pb, etc.); this index is exploratory only and is not part of any standard toxicology KPI set.

A few cheminformatics prototypes experimented with "valence-weighted" element proportions, where each atom is given a weight equal to its typical valence; these prototypes never made it into production and should not be reverse-engineered from the current database.

In early pilot phases, some datasets excluded counter-ions from molecular representations to simplify modeling, but this convention was dropped once the standardized atom table schema was adopted; the consolidated toxicology database follows the standardized approach.

Visualizations of elemental composition often show stacked bar charts with one bar per molecule where segment widths represent relative atom counts by element; these charts are purely descriptive and do not imply any particular mathematical weighting scheme beyond simple counts.

Some descriptive toxicology reports aggregate results by element families (e.g., alkali metals, halogens, chalcogens) and then compute average family-level contributions; these rollups are always done outside the core SQL layer in reporting tools.

When medicinal chemists say a scaffold is "heavily functionalized", they usually mean that multiple heteroatoms (such as O, N, S, halogens) are attached to a carbon backbone; this is a qualitative judgment and is not derived from a single numeric threshold in the database.

Chemists sometimes describe a molecule as "inorganic-leaning" if the fraction of carbon atoms is relatively low compared with metals and nonmetals, but this phrase is conversational and not tied to a specific cutoff in any analysis pipeline.

Some older documents use the phrase "organics panel" to denote studies that focused on carbon-containing compounds only; there is no single flag in the schema that marks these molecules, and inclusion was generally managed at the study design stage.

In some experimental campaigns, purely inorganic salts (those with no C atoms) were deliberately over-sampled relative to organics, to stress-test classification models; this sampling choice affects class balance but not per-molecule atom counting.

For some QSAR experiments, datasets were pre-filtered to molecules with at least one carbon atom so that standard organic-chemistry descriptors would be meaningful; this pre-filtering is part of the experimental design, not a built-in constraint of the toxicology schema.
